topic: A person's invention is already patented by someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the patent office hummed, a grating soundtrack to my mounting fury. I slammed my hand onto the polished mahogany desk, the sound echoing sharply in the quiet room. "Impossible," I hissed, my voice tight with disbelief. The documents spread before me—the official seal, the date, the name of the patent holder. It was *mine*. My sleepless nights, the countless prototypes, the sheer, agonizing brilliance of the mechanism I’d conceived, all reduced to a bureaucratic footnote owned by some smug legal mind. I snatched the papers, the edges crinkling under my grip, and shoved them back onto the desk with such force that a pen skittered across the surface. My jaw was clenched so tightly I could taste copper. This wasn't just a setback; it felt like a deliberate, calculated theft. I stood up, pacing the small space, my footsteps heavy and uneven, a low growl building in my chest. I needed to find the loophole, the flaw in their claim, something to tear apart this edifice of ownership.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, the digital ink of the patent registration glowing mockingly. My breath hitched, a sharp, shallow intake of air that did nothing to quell the churning in my gut. Every line of code, every schematic drawing I’d painstakingly developed over three years, was now theirs. A slow, hot flush crept up my neck, staining my cheeks crimson. "They can't," I muttered, the words sounding brittle and thin. I leaned closer to the monitor, my knuckles white where I gripped the edge of the desk. My eyes darted back and forth across the legal jargon, searching for a misplaced comma, a technicality I hadn't anticipated. A sudden, violent tremor ran through my hands, and I slammed my fist down, not on the desk this time, but against the cool metal casing of the computer tower. The resulting thud vibrated through my bones, a physical manifestation of the sheer injustice flooding my system. I needed to scream, to shatter something, but all that escaped was a ragged, strained exhale.

[story 3]

The silence in the lab was deafening, amplifying the frantic pounding of my heart against my ribs. I walked to the window, looking out at the indifferent cityscape, feeling a cold, hard knot tighten in my stomach. It wasn't just the loss of the patent; it was the dismissal of my entire vision. I turned back, my face contorted. "You think you can just take it?" The question was barely a whisper, laced with a venom that felt dangerously close to breaking. I began to pace the perimeter of the room, my movements jerky and uncontrolled, hands flexing involuntarily at my sides. I stopped abruptly, spinning around to face the empty workbench. I kicked a stray tool across the floor; it skittered across the concrete with a pathetic little sound. A wave of intense frustration washed over me, making my vision swim slightly. I clenched my fists until the skin around my knuckles turned stark white, the pressure building until I felt a strange, buzzing tension radiating from my core. I had to find a way to reclaim what was stolen.
